---
editor:
    render-on-save: true
csl: ieee.csl
format:
  html:
    filters: [tikz.lua, strip-yaml-frontmatter.lua, maintext-filter.lua, sitext-filter.lua]
---

## Biological-Level Differential Expression {#sec-diffexp-biological}

The differential expression framework developed in
@sec-diffexp-nonparametric and @sec-diffexp-eb operates entirely in the
**compositional** (CLR) space. While this is the correct statistical object for
testing compositional hypotheses, it inherits the well-known pathologies of
log-ratio transformations near the simplex boundary. Genes with very low
expression occupy a region of the simplex where even tiny absolute perturbations
in proportion produce dramatic CLR swings, because the logarithm amplifies small
values. As a result, genes that are barely expressed can appear as strongly
differentially expressed in CLR space when, in fact, their biological
distributions have hardly changed.

In this section, we derive three complementary metrics that quantify
differential expression directly in the space of the **biological**
(denoised) negative binomial distribution, completely bypassing the
compositional simplex. Because the posterior samples of the NB parameters
$(r_g, p_g)$ are already computed during the CLR pipeline, these metrics come
at near-zero additional computational cost.

### Recap: the biological distribution {#sec-diffexp-bio-recap}

From @sec-denoising, the model learns gene-specific NB parameters $(r_g, p_g)$
for each condition. The true (pre-capture) transcript count for gene $g$ follows

$$
m_g \sim \text{NB}(r_g, p_g),
$${#eq-diffexp-bio-nb}

with mean and variance

$$
\mu_g = \frac{r_g(1 - p_g)}{p_g}, \qquad
\text{Var}(m_g) = \frac{r_g(1 - p_g)}{p_g^2} = \frac{\mu_g}{p_g}.
$${#eq-diffexp-bio-mean-var}

These are the *biological* moments: they describe the true expression of gene $g$
in a cell before any technical noise is introduced. Unlike the compositional
proportions $\rho_g$, the means $\mu_g$ live in $\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ and are
not subject to closure.

### Metric 1: Biological log-fold change {#sec-diffexp-bio-lfc}

The most natural measure of mean expression change is the **biological
log-fold change**,

$$
\text{LFC}_g = \log\!\left(\frac{\mu_g^A}{\mu_g^B}\right)
= \log\!\left(\frac{r_g^A (1 - p_g^A) / p_g^A}
                     {r_g^B (1 - p_g^B) / p_g^B}\right).
$${#eq-diffexp-bio-lfc}

This is the log-ratio of the NB means between conditions A and B. Its key
advantage over the CLR difference $\Delta_g = \text{CLR}(\boldsymbol\rho^A)_g -
\text{CLR}(\boldsymbol\rho^B)_g$ is that it is **free of compositional
closure**: a gene whose mean expression does not change will have $\text{LFC}_g
= 0$ regardless of what other genes do.

#### Posterior inference

We have $N$ paired posterior draws $(r_g^{A,(s)}, p_g^{A,(s)})$ and
$(r_g^{B,(s)}, p_g^{B,(s)})$ for $s = 1, \ldots, N$. For each draw, compute

$$
\text{LFC}_g^{(s)} = \log\!\left(\frac{r_g^{A,(s)} (1 - p_g^{A,(s)}) / p_g^{A,(s)}}
                                        {r_g^{B,(s)} (1 - p_g^{B,(s)}) / p_g^{B,(s)}}\right).
$${#eq-diffexp-bio-lfc-samples}

The posterior summaries then follow the same recipe as in
@sec-diffexp-nonparametric. The posterior mean is

$$
\widehat{\text{LFC}}_g = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{s=1}^N \text{LFC}_g^{(s)},
$${#eq-diffexp-bio-lfc-mean}

and the posterior standard deviation is

$$
s_g^{\text{LFC}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{s=1}^N
\left(\text{LFC}_g^{(s)} - \widehat{\text{LFC}}_g\right)^2}.
$${#eq-diffexp-bio-lfc-sd}

The **local false sign rate** (lfsr) is the posterior probability that the sign
of $\text{LFC}_g$ has been incorrectly assigned,

$$
\text{lfsr}_g^{\text{LFC}} = \min\!\left(
  \hat{\pi}_g^+, \; 1 - \hat{\pi}_g^+
\right),
\qquad
\hat{\pi}_g^+ = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{s=1}^N \mathbf{1}\!\left[\text{LFC}_g^{(s)} > 0\right].
$${#eq-diffexp-bio-lfc-lfsr}

For practical significance with threshold $\tau_{\text{LFC}} > 0$, we define the
directional probabilities

$$
P_g^{\uparrow} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{s=1}^N \mathbf{1}\!\left[\text{LFC}_g^{(s)} > \tau_{\text{LFC}}\right],
\qquad
P_g^{\downarrow} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{s=1}^N \mathbf{1}\!\left[\text{LFC}_g^{(s)} < -\tau_{\text{LFC}}\right],
$${#eq-diffexp-bio-lfc-directional}

and the probability of a practical effect

$$
P_g^{\text{effect}} = P_g^{\uparrow} + P_g^{\downarrow}.
$${#eq-diffexp-bio-lfc-peffect}

The practical-significance lfsr is

$$
\text{lfsr}_g^{\tau} = 1 - \max(P_g^{\uparrow}, P_g^{\downarrow}).
$${#eq-diffexp-bio-lfc-lfsr-tau}


### Metric 2: Log-variance ratio {#sec-diffexp-bio-lvr}

Two conditions can have identical means yet differ in their variability.
A gene that switches from tight regulation to bursty expression may have
$\mu_g^A \approx \mu_g^B$ but vastly different variances. From
@eq-diffexp-bio-mean-var, the NB variance depends on *both* $r_g$ and $p_g$.

We define the **log-variance ratio**,

$$
\text{LVR}_g = \log\!\left(\frac{\text{Var}^A(m_g)}{\text{Var}^B(m_g)}\right)
= \log\!\left(\frac{r_g^A (1 - p_g^A) / (p_g^A)^2}
                     {r_g^B (1 - p_g^B) / (p_g^B)^2}\right).
$${#eq-diffexp-bio-lvr}

This can be decomposed as

$$
\text{LVR}_g = \text{LFC}_g + \log\!\left(\frac{p_g^B}{p_g^A}\right),
$${#eq-diffexp-bio-lvr-decomp}

which shows that the log-variance ratio captures *both* the mean shift and an
additional component from the change in $p_g$.
A gene with $\text{LFC}_g \approx 0$ but $\text{LVR}_g \neq 0$ has undergone a
pure change in overdispersion — the "shape" of the expression distribution
changed without its centre moving.

#### Posterior inference

Per-sample computation mirrors the LFC:

$$
\text{LVR}_g^{(s)} = \log\!\left(\frac{r_g^{A,(s)} (1 - p_g^{A,(s)}) / (p_g^{A,(s)})^2}
                                        {r_g^{B,(s)} (1 - p_g^{B,(s)}) / (p_g^{B,(s)})^2}\right),
$${#eq-diffexp-bio-lvr-samples}

and all summary statistics — posterior mean, SD, lfsr, $P^{\uparrow}$,
$P^{\downarrow}$, $P^{\text{effect}}$, and $\text{lfsr}_g^{\tau}$ — follow
identically to @sec-diffexp-bio-lfc with the obvious substitutions.


### Metric 3: Gamma Jeffreys divergence {#sec-diffexp-bio-kl}

The LFC and LVR each capture one moment of the distribution. A more holistic
measure asks: **how different are the two NB distributions in total?**

#### The Poisson–Gamma representation

A standard result (used extensively in @sec-dirichlet-multinomial) is that the
negative binomial admits a **Poisson–Gamma mixture** representation. If

$$
\lambda_g \sim \text{Gamma}\!\left(r_g, \; \frac{p_g}{1 - p_g}\right),
\qquad
m_g \mid \lambda_g \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda_g),
$${#eq-diffexp-bio-poisson-gamma}

then marginally $m_g \sim \text{NB}(r_g, p_g)$. Here we parameterise the Gamma
with shape $\alpha = r_g$ and rate $\beta = p_g / (1 - p_g)$, so that
$\langle \lambda_g \rangle = r_g (1 - p_g)/p_g = \mu_g$ and
$\text{Var}(\lambda_g) = r_g (1 - p_g)^2 / p_g^2$.

The latent rate $\lambda_g$ is a continuous relaxation of the NB: it captures
both the mean *and* the shape of the count distribution through the Gamma
parameters $(\alpha, \beta)$. Importantly, the **KL divergence between two
Gamma distributions has a closed form**, whereas the KL between two Negative
Binomials with different dispersion parameters does not.

#### KL divergence between Gamma distributions

Let $P = \text{Gamma}(\alpha_P, \beta_P)$ and $Q = \text{Gamma}(\alpha_Q,
\beta_Q)$. The KL divergence from $P$ to $Q$ is

$$
\text{KL}(P \| Q) =
  (\alpha_P - \alpha_Q)\,\psi(\alpha_P)
  - \log\Gamma(\alpha_P)
  + \log\Gamma(\alpha_Q)
  + \alpha_Q\!\left(\log\beta_P - \log\beta_Q\right)
  + \alpha_P\!\left(\frac{\beta_Q}{\beta_P} - 1\right),
$${#eq-diffexp-bio-gamma-kl}

where $\psi(\cdot) = \frac{d}{d\alpha}\log\Gamma(\alpha)$ is the digamma
function. This follows from a direct computation using the Gamma density.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Derivation of the Gamma KL divergence

The Gamma density with shape $\alpha$ and rate $\beta$ is

$$
f(x; \alpha, \beta) = \frac{\beta^\alpha}{\Gamma(\alpha)} x^{\alpha - 1} e^{-\beta x}.
$${#eq-diffexp-bio-gamma-density}

The log-density is

$$
\log f(x; \alpha, \beta) = \alpha \log\beta - \log\Gamma(\alpha) + (\alpha - 1)\log x - \beta x.
$${#eq-diffexp-bio-gamma-logdensity}

The KL divergence is

$$
\text{KL}(P \| Q)
= \langle \log f(x; \alpha_P, \beta_P) - \log f(x; \alpha_Q, \beta_Q) \rangle_P.
$${#eq-diffexp-bio-kl-def}

Substituting the log-densities,

$$
\text{KL}(P \| Q)
= \langle 
  \alpha_P \log\beta_P - \log\Gamma(\alpha_P) + (\alpha_P - 1)\log x - \beta_P x
  - \alpha_Q \log\beta_Q + \log\Gamma(\alpha_Q) - (\alpha_Q - 1)\log x + \beta_Q x
\bigr \rangle_P\bigl.
$$

Grouping terms,

$$
\text{KL}(P \| Q) =
  \alpha_P \log\beta_P - \alpha_Q \log\beta_Q
  - \log\Gamma(\alpha_P) + \log\Gamma(\alpha_Q)
  + (\alpha_P - \alpha_Q)\,\langle \log x \rangle_P
  - (\beta_P - \beta_Q)\,\langle x \rangle_P.
$$

Using the standard Gamma moment identities $\langle \log x \rangle_P =
\psi(\alpha_P) - \log\beta_P$ and $\langle x \rangle_P = \alpha_P / \beta_P$,

$$
\text{KL}(P \| Q) =
  \alpha_P \log\beta_P - \alpha_Q \log\beta_Q
  - \log\Gamma(\alpha_P) + \log\Gamma(\alpha_Q)
  + (\alpha_P - \alpha_Q)(\psi(\alpha_P) - \log\beta_P)
  - (\beta_P - \beta_Q)\frac{\alpha_P}{\beta_P}.
$$

Expanding,

\begin{align}
\text{KL}(P \| Q) &=
  \alpha_P \log\beta_P - \alpha_Q \log\beta_Q
  - \log\Gamma(\alpha_P) + \log\Gamma(\alpha_Q) \\
  &\quad + (\alpha_P - \alpha_Q)\psi(\alpha_P)
  - (\alpha_P - \alpha_Q)\log\beta_P \\
  &\quad - \alpha_P + \alpha_P\frac{\beta_Q}{\beta_P}.
\end{align}

The $\alpha_P \log\beta_P$ and $-\alpha_P\log\beta_P$ cancel, and $-\alpha_Q\log\beta_Q + \alpha_Q\log\beta_P = \alpha_Q(\log\beta_P - \log\beta_Q)$. Collecting,

$$
\text{KL}(P \| Q) =
  (\alpha_P - \alpha_Q)\,\psi(\alpha_P)
  - \log\Gamma(\alpha_P) + \log\Gamma(\alpha_Q)
  + \alpha_Q(\log\beta_P - \log\beta_Q)
  + \alpha_P\!\left(\frac{\beta_Q}{\beta_P} - 1\right),
$$

which is @eq-diffexp-bio-gamma-kl. $\square$
:::


#### Jeffreys divergence (symmetrisation)

The KL divergence is asymmetric: $\text{KL}(P \| Q) \neq \text{KL}(Q \| P)$ in
general. For a symmetric measure, we use the **Jeffreys divergence**,

$$
J_g = \text{KL}(P_g^A \| P_g^B) + \text{KL}(P_g^B \| P_g^A),
$${#eq-diffexp-bio-jeffreys}

where $P_g^A = \text{Gamma}(r_g^A, \beta_g^A)$ and $P_g^B =
\text{Gamma}(r_g^B, \beta_g^B)$ with $\beta_g = p_g / (1 - p_g)$.

The Jeffreys divergence satisfies $J_g \geq 0$ with equality if and only if
$P_g^A = P_g^B$, and is symmetric by construction. It captures *any*
distributional shift — changes in mean, variance, skewness, or shape — in a
single scalar.

#### Posterior inference

For each posterior draw $s$, we compute

$$
J_g^{(s)} = \text{KL}(\text{Gamma}(r_g^{A,(s)}, \beta_g^{A,(s)}) \|
                       \text{Gamma}(r_g^{B,(s)}, \beta_g^{B,(s)}))
           + \text{KL}(\text{Gamma}(r_g^{B,(s)}, \beta_g^{B,(s)}) \|
                       \text{Gamma}(r_g^{A,(s)}, \beta_g^{A,(s)})),
$${#eq-diffexp-bio-jeffreys-samples}

where $\beta_g^{A,(s)} = p_g^{A,(s)} / (1 - p_g^{A,(s)})$, and each KL uses
@eq-diffexp-bio-gamma-kl.

Because $J_g$ is non-negative by construction, the posterior inference simplifies
compared to the signed metrics. The posterior mean and standard deviation are

$$
\hat{J}_g = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{s=1}^N J_g^{(s)},
\qquad
s_g^J = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{s=1}^N (J_g^{(s)} - \hat{J}_g)^2}.
$${#eq-diffexp-bio-jeffreys-summaries}

For practical significance with threshold $\tau_J > 0$, the probability of a
practical effect is simply

$$
P_g^{\text{effect}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{s=1}^N
\mathbf{1}[J_g^{(s)} > \tau_J].
$${#eq-diffexp-bio-jeffreys-peffect}

There is no directional decomposition because $J_g$ is always non-negative.


### Why the Gamma KL and not the NB KL? {#sec-diffexp-bio-why-gamma}

A natural question is: why not compute the KL divergence between the NB
distributions directly? The NB KL divergence between $\text{NB}(r_P, p_P)$ and
$\text{NB}(r_Q, p_Q)$ is

$$
\text{KL}(\text{NB}_{P} \| \text{NB}_{Q})
= \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \binom{k + r_P - 1}{k} p_P^{r_P}(1-p_P)^k
\log\frac{\binom{k + r_P - 1}{k} p_P^{r_P}(1-p_P)^k}
         {\binom{k + r_Q - 1}{k} p_Q^{r_Q}(1-p_Q)^k}.
$${#eq-diffexp-bio-nb-kl}

When $r_P = r_Q$, the binomial coefficient ratios cancel and the sum simplifies
to a closed form involving only $p_P$ and $p_Q$. However, when $r_P \neq r_Q$,
the ratio of binomial coefficients $\binom{k + r_P - 1}{k} / \binom{k + r_Q -
1}{k}$ does not telescope, and no tractable closed form is known. The sum must be
truncated and evaluated numerically, which is expensive and introduces
approximation error, especially for genes with large $r$.

The Gamma KL, by contrast, exploits the Poisson–Gamma representation: the Gamma
latent rate captures all of the NB's parametric structure (both its mean and its
overdispersion), and the remaining Poisson layer is a shared observation model
that does not vary between conditions. In this sense, the Gamma KL measures
exactly the divergence in the *generative mechanism* that produces the counts,
which is the quantity of biological interest.


### Decision framework: four complementary views {#sec-diffexp-bio-decision}

With the biological metrics computed, the analyst has four complementary views of
each gene:

| Metric | Space | Captures | Free of closure? |
|--------|-------|----------|------------------|
| CLR difference $\Delta_g$ | Compositional simplex | Relative abundance shift | No |
| Biological LFC | Denoised count space | Mean expression shift | Yes |
| Log-variance ratio | Denoised count space | Dispersion shift | Yes |
| Jeffreys divergence $J_g$ | Latent Gamma rate | Full distributional shift | Yes |

: Summary of the four DE metrics. {#tbl-diffexp-bio-metrics}

The recommended workflow is:

1. **Screen with CLR**: use the CLR-based lfsr (with or without empirical Bayes
   shrinkage) as the primary filter, exactly as in @sec-diffexp-nonparametric and
   @sec-diffexp-eb.
2. **Validate with biological LFC**: for genes called as DE in CLR space,
   check whether the biological LFC is consistent. Lowly expressed genes with
   large CLR effects but negligible biological LFC are likely compositional
   artefacts.
3. **Detect variance changes**: inspect the log-variance ratio for genes with
   small LFC but high Jeffreys divergence — these genes have undergone a change
   in expression noise (e.g., a switch from tight regulation to bursty
   transcription) that the mean-based metrics miss.
4. **Flag distributional shifts**: the Jeffreys divergence serves as a catch-all
   for any change in the NB distribution. Genes with high $J_g$ but low CLR
   $|\Delta_g|$ are candidates for further investigation.

No single metric is universally "best." The CLR metric is the only one that
respects the compositional nature of the data, and remains the gold standard for
testing compositional hypotheses. The biological metrics complement it by
providing a non-compositional check that is especially valuable for lowly
expressed genes where the CLR transform amplifies noise.


### Robustness of CLR inference to capture probability misspecification {#sec-diffexp-bio-robustness}

The recommended workflow above places the CLR difference as the primary screen
for differential expression, with the biological-level metrics serving a
validation role. A natural concern is: **what happens to CLR-based inference when
the capture probability $\nu_c$ is not estimated correctly?** As discussed in
@sec-cap-identifiability, the observed library size constrains only the product
$\nu_c \cdot \mu_T$, and resolving this degeneracy requires external information
(the biology-informed prior on total mRNA, @sec-capture-prior). If that anchor is
imperfect---for instance, because the true total mRNA content $M_c$ differs from
the assumed $M_0$---one might worry that all downstream inferences are
compromised.

In this section, we show that CLR-based differential expression enjoys a
remarkable **cancellation property** that makes it robust to such
misspecification. The argument proceeds in three steps: (1) a wrong capture
probability produces a uniform scaling of the Dirichlet parameters; (2) CLR
means are exactly invariant to such scaling; and (3) CLR variances are only
mildly affected in the large-concentration regime typical of scRNA-seq data. In
contrast, the biological-level metrics developed earlier in this section absorb
the scaling error directly and lose their absolute interpretation.

#### Step 1: From misspecified $\nu_c$ to uniformly scaled Dirichlet parameters

Suppose the true capture probability is $\nu_c$ but the model infers a
misspecified value $\tilde{\nu}_c \neq \nu_c$. We analyze the consequences under
the mean-odds parameterization, where the biological parameters are $\mu_g$ (the
mean expression of gene $g$) and $\phi$ (the shared odds ratio controlling
overdispersion), with negative binomial parameters $r_g = \mu_g \cdot \phi$ and
$p = 1 / (1 + \phi)$ (see @eq-mean-nb-params).

Recall from @sec-cap-identifiability that the expected library size is
$\langle L_c \rangle = \nu_c \cdot \mu_T$ (@eq-cap-expected-lib), so the data
constrain the product $\nu_c \cdot \mu_T$ but not each factor individually. When
the model adopts $\tilde{\nu}_c$ instead of the true $\nu_c$, it must compensate
to fit the same observed counts. Define the misspecification ratio

$$
\kappa = \frac{\nu_c}{\tilde{\nu}_c}.
$${#eq-diffexp-rob-kappa}

Since the observed library size is fixed, the constraint
$\tilde{\nu}_c \cdot \tilde{\mu}_T = \nu_c \cdot \mu_T$ forces the model to
adopt a rescaled total biological expression:

$$
\tilde{\mu}_T = \kappa \cdot \mu_T.
$${#eq-diffexp-rob-mu-total}

Under the shared-$\phi$ assumption, this rescaling is distributed uniformly
across all genes. The effective $\hat{p}$ in the likelihood (see
@eq-cap-phat-reminder) adjusts to absorb the capture probability change, which
in the mean-odds parameterization translates to a rescaled $\phi$:

$$
\tilde{\phi} = \kappa \cdot \phi.
$${#eq-diffexp-rob-phi-tilde}

Since $r_g = \mu_g \cdot \phi$, the Dirichlet parameters that govern normalized
expression (@eq-norm-dirichlet-conditional) are uniformly rescaled:

$$
\tilde{r}_g = \tilde{\mu}_g \cdot \tilde{\phi} = \kappa \cdot \mu_g \cdot \kappa \cdot \phi = \kappa^2 \cdot r_g, \quad \text{for all } g.
$${#eq-diffexp-rob-r-scaled}

The essential point is that this rescaling is **gene-independent**: every
$r_g$ is multiplied by the same constant $\kappa^2$. This uniformity is a
direct consequence of the shared-$p$ (or shared-$\phi$) structure of the model.

#### Step 2: CLR means are exactly invariant to uniform scaling

We now show that the CLR transformation annihilates any uniform multiplicative
factor on the Dirichlet parameters. Let $c > 0$ be an arbitrary positive
constant (in our case, $c = \kappa^2$), and define $\tilde{r}_g = c \cdot r_g$
for all $g = 1, \ldots, D$.

The Dirichlet mean proportions (@eq-norm-dirichlet-mean) are

$$
\tilde{\rho}_g = \frac{\tilde{r}_g}{\tilde{r}_T}
= \frac{c \cdot r_g}{c \cdot r_T}
= \frac{r_g}{r_T}
= \rho_g,
$${#eq-diffexp-rob-rho-invariant}

where $\tilde{r}_T = \sum_j \tilde{r}_j = c \cdot r_T$. The proportions are
completely unchanged. Therefore, the CLR coordinates
(@eq-diffexp-clr-definition) applied to the Dirichlet mean are identical under
the true and misspecified models.

To see the cancellation explicitly at the CLR level, consider applying the CLR
transformation directly to the Dirichlet parameters (rather than to the
proportions). The CLR coordinate for gene $g$ evaluated on
$\log \underline{\tilde{r}}$ is

$$
\widetilde{z}_{\text{CLR},g}
= \log(\tilde{r}_g) - \frac{1}{D} \sum_{j=1}^{D} \log(\tilde{r}_j)
= \log(c \cdot r_g) - \frac{1}{D} \sum_{j=1}^{D} \log(c \cdot r_j).
$${#eq-diffexp-rob-clr-expand}

Expanding each logarithm:

$$
\widetilde{z}_{\text{CLR},g}
= \log c + \log r_g - \frac{1}{D} \sum_{j=1}^{D} (\log c + \log r_j)
= \log c + \log r_g - \log c - \frac{1}{D} \sum_{j=1}^{D} \log r_j.
$${#eq-diffexp-rob-clr-cancel}

The $\log c$ terms cancel exactly, leaving

$$
\widetilde{z}_{\text{CLR},g} = \log r_g - \frac{1}{D} \sum_{j=1}^{D} \log r_j = z_{\text{CLR},g}.
$${#eq-diffexp-rob-clr-invariant}

This is a direct consequence of the centering matrix $\underline{\underline{C}}$
annihilating the constant vector $\underline{1}$
(@eq-diffexp-centering-orthogonal): the uniform shift $\log c \cdot
\underline{1}$ lies in the null space of $\underline{\underline{C}}$ and is
projected away.

**For CLR-based differential expression, the implication is immediate.** The CLR
difference between two conditions,

$$
\Delta_g = z_{\text{CLR},g}^A - z_{\text{CLR},g}^B,
$${#eq-diffexp-rob-delta}

depends only on the ratios of the Dirichlet parameters within each condition.
Any gene-independent scaling error in $\nu_c$ cancels out of $\Delta_g$
entirely. The posterior mean of $\Delta_g$---and hence the sign, ranking, and
relative magnitudes of all gene-level CLR effects---is **exactly preserved**
under capture probability misspecification.

#### Step 3: CLR variances are mildly affected (second order)

While the CLR *means* are exactly invariant, the CLR *variances* do depend on
the absolute scale of the Dirichlet parameters through the concentration
parameter $r_T = \sum_g r_g$.

Recall from @eq-norm-dirichlet-variance that the variance of the Dirichlet
marginal is

$$
\text{Var}(\rho_g \mid \underline{r}) = \frac{r_g(r_T - r_g)}{r_T^2(r_T + 1)}.
$${#eq-diffexp-rob-dir-var-recall}

Under the rescaling $\tilde{r}_g = c \cdot r_g$ (with $\tilde{r}_T = c \cdot r_T$),
this becomes

$$
\text{Var}(\tilde{\rho}_g \mid \underline{\tilde{r}})
= \frac{(c \cdot r_g)(c \cdot r_T - c \cdot r_g)}{(c \cdot r_T)^2(c \cdot r_T + 1)}
= \frac{c^2 \cdot r_g(r_T - r_g)}{c^2 \cdot r_T^2 \cdot (c \cdot r_T + 1)}.
$${#eq-diffexp-rob-dir-var-scaled-step}

Simplifying:

$$
\text{Var}(\tilde{\rho}_g \mid \underline{\tilde{r}})
= \frac{r_g(r_T - r_g)}{r_T^2(c \cdot r_T + 1)}
= \text{Var}(\rho_g \mid \underline{r}) \cdot \frac{r_T + 1}{c \cdot r_T + 1}.
$${#eq-diffexp-rob-dir-var-ratio}

The ratio of the true variance to the misspecified variance is therefore

$$
\frac{\text{Var}(\tilde{\rho}_g)}{\text{Var}(\rho_g)}
= \frac{r_T + 1}{c \cdot r_T + 1}.
$${#eq-diffexp-rob-var-ratio}

For $c > 1$ (the model overestimates total expression, i.e., underestimates
$\nu_c$), the variance decreases; for $c < 1$, the variance increases. However,
in the large-concentration regime $r_T \gg 1$ that is typical of scRNA-seq data
(where $r_T = \sum_g r_g$ sums over tens of thousands of genes), this ratio
satisfies

$$
\frac{r_T + 1}{c \cdot r_T + 1}
= \frac{1}{c} \cdot \frac{1 + 1/r_T}{1 + 1/(c \cdot r_T)}
\approx \frac{1}{c}\left(1 + \frac{c - 1}{c \cdot r_T}\right)
= \frac{1}{c} + O\!\left(\frac{1}{r_T}\right).
$${#eq-diffexp-rob-var-asymptotic}

The leading-order effect is a gene-independent scaling by $1/c$, which shifts all
CLR variances by the same multiplicative factor. This uniform shift does **not**
change the relative ranking of genes by their z-scores or lfsr values: if gene
$g$ has a larger $|\Delta_g| / \text{sd}_g$ than gene $h$ under the true model,
it will still have a larger ratio under the misspecified model (since both
standard deviations are scaled by the same factor $1/\sqrt{c}$). The $O(1/r_T)$
correction is the only gene-specific perturbation, and it vanishes as
$r_T \to \infty$.

In summary: **the CLR mean differences are exactly invariant, and the CLR
z-score rankings are preserved up to a negligible $O(1/r_T)$ correction.** The
lfsr values and PEFP thresholds computed from CLR coordinates are therefore
robust to moderate errors in the capture probability scaling.

#### Contrast with biological-level metrics

The cancellation that protects CLR inference does **not** extend to the
biological-level metrics developed earlier in this section.

**Biological LFC** (@eq-diffexp-bio-lfc). The biological log-fold change is
$\text{LFC}_g = \log(\mu_g^A / \mu_g^B)$. Under misspecification with
condition-specific ratios $\kappa^A$ and $\kappa^B$, the inferred means become
$\tilde{\mu}_g^A = \kappa^A \cdot \mu_g^A$ and
$\tilde{\mu}_g^B = \kappa^B \cdot \mu_g^B$, giving

$$
\widetilde{\text{LFC}}_g = \log\!\left(\frac{\kappa^A \cdot \mu_g^A}
{\kappa^B \cdot \mu_g^B}\right)
= \text{LFC}_g + \log\!\left(\frac{\kappa^A}{\kappa^B}\right).
$${#eq-diffexp-rob-lfc-bias}

Every gene's LFC is shifted by the same additive offset
$\log(\kappa^A / \kappa^B)$. The *sign* and *ranking* of LFC values are
preserved (since the offset is gene-independent), but the *absolute magnitudes*
are biased. This means that practical significance thresholds
$|\text{LFC}_g| > \tau$ will include or exclude different genes depending on the
direction and size of the offset.

**Log-variance ratio** (@eq-diffexp-bio-lvr). The LVR inherits the same
additive bias as the LFC and picks up an additional term from the change in
$\phi$. Both are gene-independent, so rankings are again preserved but absolute
values are shifted.

**Jeffreys divergence** (@eq-diffexp-bio-jeffreys). The Gamma rate parameter
$\beta_g = 1/\phi_g$ absorbs the capture scaling nonlinearly. While the
divergence is not simply shifted by an additive offset, it is systematically
inflated or deflated for all genes, again distorting absolute magnitudes without
necessarily changing rankings.

This contrast reinforces the decision framework in @sec-diffexp-bio-decision:
**use CLR as the primary screen** (where inference is robust to capture
misspecification), then **validate with biological metrics** (where absolute
values should be interpreted cautiously when the capture model is uncertain).

#### The mixture model case

A particularly important setting is a **mixture model** where different cell
types (components) are jointly modeled. Different cell types may have genuinely
different total mRNA contents $M_c$, so a single $M_0$ cannot accurately anchor
all components simultaneously. In practice, this manifests as different mixture
components converging to different effective capture probability scalings.

The cancellation argument above applies **per-component**: within each mixture
component $k$, the capture misspecification ratio $\kappa^{(k)}$ is absorbed
into a uniform rescaling of that component's Dirichlet parameters, and the CLR
coordinates within component $k$ are exactly invariant. The CLR difference
between conditions A and B within the same component,

$$
\Delta_g^{(k)} = z_{\text{CLR},g}^{A,(k)} - z_{\text{CLR},g}^{B,(k)},
$${#eq-diffexp-rob-mixture-delta}

is therefore robust even when different components learn different $\kappa^{(k)}$
values. The key requirement is that the scaling be uniform *within* each
component---which is guaranteed by the shared-$\phi$ model structure---not that
it be the same *across* components.

This property is especially reassuring for mixture models where the
biology-informed prior on $M_0$ may conflict with the true total mRNA of one or
more cell types. Even if the $M_0$ anchor is imperfect and the model compensates
by adjusting the effective capture scaling differently for each component, the
CLR-based differential expression analysis within each component remains valid.
The biological-level metrics, by contrast, would reflect the component-specific
scaling errors and require careful interpretation.


### Caveat: the NB parameter-space boundary {#sec-diffexp-bio-caveat}

While the biological metrics are free of compositional closure, they have their
own boundary artifact.  For genes with near-zero biological expression, the
Gamma rate $\beta_g \to \infty$ regardless of parameterization.  In this regime,
tiny and **unidentifiable** posterior fluctuations produce enormous $\beta_g$
ratios between conditions, inflating all three biological metrics.

The severity of this artifact depends on the model's parameterization:

- **Canonical / mean_prob** ($p$ sampled directly): the Gamma rate is
  $\beta_g = p_g / (1 - p_g)$.  As $p_g \to 1$, the $(1 - p_g)$ subtraction
  suffers from catastrophic cancellation in finite-precision arithmetic (float32),
  amplifying the instability beyond what the mathematical singularity alone would
  produce.
- **Mean_odds** ($\phi$ sampled directly): the Gamma rate is $\beta_g = 1/\phi_g$.
  As $\phi_g \to 0$ the rate still diverges, but the computation $1/\phi$ is a
  simple reciprocal with **no subtraction**, so it avoids the catastrophic
  cancellation that plagues the $p/(1-p)$ form.

The LFC similarly benefits from the `mean_odds` parameterization: since $\mu_g$
is sampled directly, the biological LFC $\log(\mu_A / \mu_B)$ never involves
the numerically fragile $r(1-p)/p$ reconstruction.

**Recommendation:** use the `mean_odds` parameterization for models where
biological DE metrics will be computed.  The pipeline automatically detects
the parameterization and selects the most stable computation path.  Regardless
of parameterization, **filter genes by minimum biological expression** before
interpreting the results.  The `max_bio_expr` field in the output (defined as
$\max(\hat{\mu}_g^A, \hat{\mu}_g^B)$) is provided for exactly this purpose.
A threshold of $\mu_{\min} \approx 0.1$--$1.0$ typically suffices.


### Parameterization-aware computation {#sec-diffexp-bio-param}

The biological DE pipeline selects the most numerically stable formulae based
on which posterior samples are available:

| Parameterization | $\mu_g$ | $\text{var}_g$ | $\beta_g$ (Gamma rate) |
|---|---|---|---|
| `mean_odds` | sampled | $\mu_g (1 + \phi_g)$ | $1 / \phi_g$ |
| `mean_prob` | sampled | $\mu_g / p_g$ | $p_g / (1 - p_g)$ |
| `canonical` | $r_g (1 - p_g) / p_g$ | $\mu_g / p_g$ | $p_g / (1 - p_g)$ |

The three tiers are selected automatically — the user does not need to specify
anything beyond using results objects.


### Implementation notes {#sec-diffexp-bio-implementation}

All four metrics are computed by default when calling `compare()` with
`method="empirical"` or `method="shrinkage"`. The biological metrics are
available via the `.biological_level()` method on the results object:

```python
de = scribe.de.compare(
    results_A, results_B,
    method="shrinkage",
    component_A=0, component_B=0,
)

# CLR-based DE (as before)
clr_results = de.gene_level(tau=jnp.log(1.5))

# Biological-level DE
bio_results = de.biological_level(
    tau_lfc=jnp.log(1.5),
    tau_var=jnp.log(2.0),
    tau_kl=0.5,
)
```

When results objects are passed to `compare()`, the pipeline auto-extracts
`mu` and `phi` samples from `posterior_samples` alongside the standard `r` and
`p`.  These native-parameterization samples are stored in the results object
and used by `biological_level()` to select the best computation path (see
@sec-diffexp-bio-param). Setting `compute_biological=False` in `compare()`
disables the storage of all these samples, saving memory when only CLR metrics
are needed.

To filter out genes with unreliable metrics (see @sec-diffexp-bio-caveat):

```python
# Filter genes with near-zero expression in both conditions
reliable = bio_results["max_bio_expr"] > 0.1
bio_lfc_filtered = bio_results["lfc_mean"][reliable]
bio_kl_filtered = bio_results["kl_mean"][reliable]
```
